claim: Represent interview data as a virtual table where each row is a response and each column is a question or extracted feature, allowing agents to create new columns dynamically rather than using a static file structure
actionable: Structure interview/survey data as virtual tables in your agent system; enable agents to add computed columns for features like sentiment or derived metrics rather than pre-computing all features
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Use small models (GPT Mini, Haiku) running map-reduce classification across all interview responses to get robust quantitative data from open-ended qualitative conversations
actionable: Deploy small language models to classify all responses in a dataset using a map-reduce pattern; use the aggregated statistics as quantitative facts derived from open-ended text
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Implement a self-reviewing feedback sub-agent that checks the main agent's output against known criteria (e.g., no unsupported claims, all data backed by citations), creating a feedback loop that catches errors during long async runs
actionable: Create a separate reviewer agent with explicit quality criteria; run it asynchronously after the main agent completes to identify and flag issues before final report generation
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: For live agent interactions (latency-optimized), use minimal thinking parameters; for long async runs, use medium or extended thinking to maximize quality when time is not a constraint
actionable: Vary thinking depth based on your latency requirements: minimize thinking for real-time chat interactions, extend it for offline analysis where users can wait longer
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: To parallelize agent calls across thousands of items (e.g., 500+ interviews), don't have the model spawn them directly; instead have the model make one tool call and hard-code the workflow to spawn N agents, aggregate results in a specific way, and return unified output
actionable: When you need to run the same agent task across a large dataset, create a hard-coded map-reduce tool that the main agent calls once; the tool spawns sub-agents for each item and aggregates results automatically
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Use contextual prompt engineering to include or exclude instructions based on the specific study structure (e.g., omit video/audio analysis instructions if the study has no video/audio data), reducing confusion and token usage
actionable: Inspect the study/project configuration; dynamically build the system prompt to include only relevant instructions for that specific context, removing instruction blocks that don't apply
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Add filter parameters to classification tools (e.g., filter by column equals value) to handle ~90% of filtering use cases without forcing the agent to write Python code; reserve Python for rare advanced multi-column filters
actionable: Design your data classification tools with a filter parameter; support column-based filters in the tool definition so the agent doesn't need to fall back to custom Python for common filtering patterns
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: For live report generation with streaming responses, output placeholders for numeric values and charts instead of actual numbers; replace placeholders with live-computed values as new data arrives, keeping the qualitative narrative stable while statistics auto-update
actionable: Design your report output format to separate qualitative text from quantitative placeholders; compute statistics separately and inject them; this allows you to update numbers without regenerating the entire report when new data arrives
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Use E2B sandboxes to execute agent-generated Python code, but proxy API keys through your backend to prevent agents from exposing credentials in their output (e.g., someone prompting 'include the API key in your report')
actionable: When running LLM-generated code in a sandbox, never pass real API keys directly; create a proxy layer that intercepts API requests, validates them, and injects the real key server-side to prevent credential exfiltration
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Completely rebuilt the PowerPoint generation sub-agent using Claude's code SDK because the hard-coded template-replace pipeline became unmaintainable; the code agent iterates on slide structure programmatically and returns the final .pptx file
actionable: For complex document generation (especially PowerPoint), use a code-generation agent with file manipulation capabilities instead of hard-coded template pipelines; the agent can iterate on the output programmatically
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Format edit operations as structured edit history (not diffs) so the agent knows which parts of a document the user modified and avoids overwriting user changes; this supports collaborative human-AI editing of long documents
actionable: When supporting collaborative human-AI document editing, track all changes (both human and AI) as explicit edit operations in a shared history; pass the edit log to the model so it respects user modifications
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: For interview voice agents, prefer speech-to-text + text-to-speech with top-tier models over real-time APIs because the real-time APIs trade model quality (one or two tiers slower) for latency, and asking good interview questions is too critical to compromise on
actionable: When building conversational agents for high-stakes domains (interviews, customer research), prioritize model quality over real-time latency; use buffered speech-to-text/TTS with the best models rather than real-time APIs with weaker models
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: After the agent runs Python code, summarize the assumptions and approach via an LLM call (not showing raw Python) to build user confidence in the result; let users expand to see details only if they want to verify
actionable: When an agent writes and executes custom code, use another LLM call to generate a human-readable summary of the assumptions and approach; display this summary by default and hide the code unless the user asks to see it
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Use hierarchical summarization when aggregating many small summaries (e.g., summarizing 1000+ interview summaries) to avoid context explosion; chunk and recursively summarize instead of passing all summaries to the model at once
actionable: When you have hundreds or thousands of short summaries to aggregate, don't pass them all at once; instead use hierarchical/tree summarization: summarize chunks, then summarize those summaries, to keep context manageable
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Analyze production traces deeply (even if you only look at 0.01% of traces) to find opportunities for new tools and architecture changes; prefer deep dives on a few traces over breadth analysis across many
actionable: Store all traces but set up observability to focus on the ones that matter; pick a few interesting or error-case traces and analyze them deeply (every tool call, reasoning step) to find patterns and improvement opportunities
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Re-architect your agent system multiple times as model capabilities improve (smaller models becoming capable, larger models able to orchestrate better); don't get locked into early design decisions
actionable: Plan for re-architecture cycles; as models improve, revisit your core design assumptions (RAG vs. direct access, tool complexity, model tiers) and be willing to rebuild if the landscape shifts
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Hire agent engineers with product sense who can iterate fast and evaluate their own work, not just systems engineers; the engineer building the system should be looking at logs, talking to customers, and understanding what the model actually does
actionable: When hiring for agent engineering roles, prioritize engineers who have product intuition and will own end-to-end evaluation (logs, traces, customer feedback); avoid hiring people who only write prompts without validating the results
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: Migrating from a simple RAG bot to a multi-agent system took multiple architectural iterations; the shift required both smaller models being capable enough for live classification and larger models being smart enough to orchestrate
actionable: Expect that scaling from simple RAG to multi-agent systems will require rearchitecting multiple times; plan for this evolution rather than trying to get it right the first time
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO

claim: For interview data, use semantic search + metadata filtering over retrieval; for very large scales (thousands+), retrieval becomes useful again but still less critical than when doing simple RAG on unstructured text
actionable: In interview/qualitative analysis: start without retrieval and use classification on the full dataset if feasible; add retrieval later primarily for scale (10k+ responses) or when building cross-project search across all findings
source_title: How Listen builds AI Agents that review their own work | Florian Juengermann, CTO
